agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Leave management module within an HR software system. Governs employee time-off accrual, request submission, approval workflow, balance enforcement, and year-end carry-over policy. This is a core HR process with direct payroll and compliance implications.","bounded_context":"Leave Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Leave Balance","definition":"The number of accrued but unused leave days available to an employee at any point in time. Cannot be negative."},{"term":"Accrual","definition":"The process by which an employee earns leave entitlement — 1.5 days per month worked."},{"term":"Leave Request","definition":"A formal submission by an employee to take time off for a specified date range, subject to balance availability and manager approval."},{"term":"Medical Leave","definition":"A category of leave triggered by illness or medical need, which is exempt from the advance-notice requirement but requires a doctor's note submitted within 5 days."},{"term":"Doctor's Note","definition":"A medical certificate that must accompany any retroactively submitted medical leave request. Required within 5 days of the leave."},{"term":"Working Days","definition":"Business days used to calculate the advance-notice period (3 working days). Excludes weekends and company holidays."},{"term":"Carry-Over Cap","definition":"The maximum number of unused leave days (10) that may be transferred to the next calendar year. Any excess is forfeited."},{"term":"Forfeiture","definition":"The permanent loss of unused leave days exceeding the carry-over cap at the calendar year boundary."},{"term":"Contract End Date","definition":"The date on which an employee's current employment contract terminates. Leave requests may not span beyond this date."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Employees accrue 1.5 days of leave per month worked.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Accrual rate is a company policy parameter. HR could change it to 2.0 days/month without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given an employee who has worked N full months, their total accrued leave equals N × 1.5 days (minus any leave already taken or forfeited)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An employee's leave balance must never go below zero. Leave cannot be taken before it is accrued.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A negative leave balance represents a physically impossible state — an employee consuming time-off they have not earned. Allowing this corrupts payroll and accrual accounting.","testable_assertion":"At no point in time shall any employee record have a leave balance < 0."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A non-medical leave request must be submitted at least 3 working days before the requested leave start date.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The advance-notice window is an operational policy. Management could shorten it to 1 day without breaking system integrity.","testable_assertion":"Given today is T, a non-medical leave request with start date S is rejected at submission time if the number of working days between T and S is less than 3."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Medical leave may be submitted retroactively, but a doctor's note must be provided within 5 days of the leave.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 5-day window and retroactive exception are policy decisions, not data integrity constraints.","testable_assertion":"A medical leave request submitted after the leave dates is accepted only if (a) a doctor's note is attached or flagged as pending, and (b) submission occurs within 5 days of the leave end date."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Only a manager may approve or reject a leave request. The employee cannot self-approve.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Separation of requester and approver is a fundamental control. Allowing self-approval breaks the authorization model and audit trail.","testable_assertion":"The approver/rejector of a leave request must be a user with the Manager role and must not be the same person as the requester."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Rejected leave does not consume leave balance.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deducting balance for rejected leave would corrupt the balance, creating a discrepancy between actual time-off taken and balance consumed.","testable_assertion":"When a leave request transitions to REJECTED status, the employee's leave balance remains unchanged from its pre-request value."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A leave request must not span across (past) the employee's contract end date.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"An employee cannot be on approved leave for a period during which they are no longer under contract. This is a legal/contractual integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an employee with contract end date D, any leave request with an end date > D must be rejected at submission time."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"At the calendar year boundary, unused leave days exceeding 10 are forfeited. A maximum of 10 days carries over.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The carry-over cap of 10 days is a company policy. A VP could raise it to 15 without breaking system integrity.","testable_assertion":"On January 1 of each year, any employee with a leave balance > 10 has their balance set to 10. The forfeited amount is logged."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An employee has worked 4 full months with no leave taken","when":"The system calculates their leave balance","then":"The balance is exactly 6.0 days (4 × 1.5)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An employee has a leave balance of 2.0 days","when":"They submit a leave request for 3.0 days","then":"The request is rejected immediately with an insufficient-balance error and balance remains 2.0"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Today is Monday and an employee submits a non-medical leave request","when":"The requested start date is Wednesday (2 working days away)","then":"The request is rejected with an insufficient-notice error"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Today is Monday and an employee submits a non-medical leave request","when":"The requested start date is Thursday (3 working days away)","then":"The request passes the notice-period validation and proceeds to manager approval"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"An employee was sick last Wednesday and it is now Monday (5 calendar days later)","when":"They submit a medical leave request for last Wednesday with a doctor's note attached","then":"The request passes validation and proceeds to manager approval"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An employee was sick last Monday and it is now the following Monday (7 days later)","when":"They submit a medical leave request for last Monday with a doctor's note","then":"The request is rejected because it exceeds the 5-day retroactive window"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An employee has a pending leave request for 3 days and a balance of 5 days","when":"A manager rejects the request","then":"The employee's leave balance remains 5 days and the request status is REJECTED"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"An employee has a pending leave request for 3 days and a balance of 5 days","when":"A manager approves the request","then":"The employee's leave balance is reduced to 2 days and the request status is APPROVED"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"An employee's contract ends on March 15","when":"They submit a leave request for March 13–17","then":"The request is rejected because it spans beyond the contract end date"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"An employee has 14 unused leave days on December 31","when":"The year-end carry-over process runs","then":"Their balance on January 1 is 10 days and 4 days are recorded as forfeited"},{"id":"AC-11","given":"An employee has 8 unused leave days on December 31","when":"The year-end carry-over process runs","then":"Their balance on January 1 remains 8 days with 0 days forfeited"},{"id":"AC-12","given":"An employee submits a leave request","when":"They attempt to approve their own request","then":"The system rejects the approval with an authorization error"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Employee starts mid-month (e.g., joins on the 15th)","expected_behavior":"Accrual for the partial first month must be defined — either prorate (e.g., 0.75 days for half a month) or accrue nothing until the first full month. The spec does not clarify this; recommend prorating."},{"scenario":"Leave request starts on a non-working day (weekend/holiday)","expected_behavior":"The system should reject the request or warn the employee, since leave on non-working days has no balance impact and suggests a user error."},{"scenario":"Medical leave submitted on exactly the 5th day","expected_behavior":"The 5-day boundary should be inclusive — submission on day 5 is accepted. Off-by-one errors here are common."},{"scenario":"Employee's contract end date falls in the middle of an already-approved leave","expected_behavior":"If the contract end date changes after approval, the system should flag or cancel the portion of leave extending beyond the new end date."},{"scenario":"Manager approves leave but balance was consumed by another concurrent approval in the interim","expected_behavior":"Balance deduction must be atomic. If two approvals race and the second would cause a negative balance, the second approval must fail with an insufficient-balance error."},{"scenario":"Employee with zero months worked submits a leave request","expected_behavior":"Balance is 0. Request is rejected for insufficient balance."},{"scenario":"Year-end carry-over when employee has a pending (unapproved) leave request","expected_behavior":"Pending requests should not reduce the balance subject to forfeiture. Only approved/taken leave reduces balance. Pending requests against forfeited balance should be auto-cancelled or flagged."},{"scenario":"Medical leave without a doctor's note — is the request held pending or rejected outright?","expected_behavior":"Recommend holding the request in a PENDING_DOCUMENTATION state with a 5-day deadline, after which it is auto-rejected if no note is provided."},{"scenario":"Leave request for exactly the contract end date (last day)","expected_behavior":"Should be permitted — the employee is still under contract on that date. The constraint is that leave must not extend PAST the end date."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect accrual or carry-over logic directly impacts payroll liability calculations. Over-accrual inflates leave liabilities on the balance sheet; under-accrual creates compliance exposure. Forfeiture miscalculations could lead to employee disputes or legal claims.","operational":"The 3-day advance notice rule protects workforce planning. Medical leave retroactivity balances employee welfare against operational predictability. Carry-over caps prevent leave hoarding that causes staffing gaps when large balances are eventually used.","user":"Employees need a transparent, predictable leave balance and clear feedback when requests are rejected. Managers need an efficient approval workflow. Poor UX around balance visibility or rejection reasons will drive HR support tickets.","risk":"Primary risks: (1) Race conditions on concurrent approvals causing negative balances, (2) Off-by-one errors on day-count boundaries (3 working days, 5-day window, year-end cutoff), (3) Timezone mismatches between employee locale and system clock affecting deadline calculations.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Employees","impact":"Direct users — accrual accuracy, request experience, and carry-over fairness affect trust and satisfaction."},{"group":"Managers","impact":"Approval workflow efficiency and visibility into team availability for workforce planning."},{"group":"HR / People Operations","impact":"Policy enforcement, audit trail integrity, dispute resolution, and compliance reporting."},{"group":"Payroll / Finance","impact":"Leave liability accounting depends on accurate balance tracking and year-end forfeiture execution."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Labor law compliance for leave entitlements varies by jurisdiction. Incorrect forfeiture or denial of medical leave could create legal exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":75,"reasoning":"The specification is well-structured with clear, non-contradictory rules. All core business rules are explicitly stated and internally consistent. Confidence is not higher because several operational details are left unspecified — partial-month accrual, the exact definition of the 5-day window (calendar vs working days), and the treatment of pending requests at year-end. These are normal spec gaps for a first-pass requirements document, not blockers.","high_confidence_areas":["Non-negative balance invariant","Advance notice requirement and medical leave exception","Rejected leave does not consume balance","Contract end date boundary constraint","Carry-over cap and forfeiture at 10 days"],"low_confidence_areas":["Partial-month accrual calculation (not specified — prorated vs. full-month-only)","Whether the 5-day medical leave window is calendar days or working days","Behavior of pending requests during year-end carry-over processing","Whether balance is reserved at request submission or deducted only at approval","Timezone and locale handling for working-day calculations"],"assumptions":["The 5-day medical leave window is measured in calendar days (the spec says 'days' without qualifier, unlike the '3 working days' advance notice which explicitly says 'working')","Leave balance is deducted upon manager approval, not at request submission","An employee cannot have multiple active contracts — contract end date is singular","The carry-over process runs exactly once at the calendar year boundary (Jan 1)","Self-approval is prohibited — a different manager must approve"]},"escalations":[],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this HR leave management domain thoroughly.

The rules are clear and well-specified. Let me break them down:

1. Accrual: 1.5 days/month
2. No negative balance (invariant)
3. 3 working days advance notice (policy)
4. Medical leave exception: retroactive within 5 days with doctor's note (policy)
5. Manager approve/reject workflow
6. Rejected leave doesn't consume balance
7. Leave can't span contract end dates (invariant)
8. Carry-over cap: max 10 days, rest forfeited (policy)


9. The spec is reasonably complete with no major contradictions, though I'm noticing several minor ambiguities worth flagging: how accrual works for partial months, which calendar defines "working days," the exact timing of year-end forfeiture, whether the 5-day medical exception window counts working or calendar days, and what leave types exist beyond medical.

These edge cases don't block implementation—the core business rules are clear enough—so I'll set confidence around 75 and document them in low_confidence_areas rather than escalate.
